case_name: Fritz Swint v. Paul Alphonse, Jr.

summary.brief: Fritz Swint v. Paul Alphonse, Jr.. Medical malpractice case involving surgery orthopaedic.

evidence_index.text: During a prolonged surgical procedure, Fritz F. Swint suffered an injury that left him with nerve damage and limited use of his right hand.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The surgery, conducted by Dr. Alphonse, lasted approximately 9 hours and 21 minutes.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr. Swint was diagnosed with compartment syndrome in his right arm the following day, and underwent surgery to relieve the pressure.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Palese testified repeatedly that when a patient is placed in the lithotomy in the steep Trendelenburg position for a lengthy surgery, the standard of care requires the physician to give the patient a positional holiday.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Michael A. Palese

evidence_index.text: Palese opined that the standard of care was breached in this case somewhere between the four and five hour mark of the surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Michael A. Palese

evidence_index.text: We reverse the order of the trial court granting summary judgment in favor of Dr. Alphonse and Midtown Urology and against Fritz and Melissa Swint.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

evidence_index.text: The trial court’s finding regarding the Swints’ insufficient evidence of causation appears to be based on two factors.
evidence_index.speaker: Court analysis

evidence_index.text: Dr. Palese further stated, however, that in his opinion, it was more likely than not that Mr. Swint would not have suffered such a significant injury had he been given a positional holiday between the four and five hour mark of surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Michael A. Palese

evidence_index.text: The Swints alleged that Dr. Alphonse committed medical malpractice by failing to reposition him during the surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court summarily found Dr. Collier’s opinion was not based on scientific principles.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient undergoing a scheduled robotic-assisted laparoscopic prostatectomy (RALP) procedure.

focused_exam.system: Pre-operative examination not detailed in judgment

critical_findings: Positioning in lithotomy with steep Trendelenburg

reveal.result_summary: Patient scheduled for robotic-assisted laparoscopic prostatectomy

timeline_phases.name: Pre-operative Assessment
timeline_phases.description: Patient assessed and prepared for robotic-assisted laparoscopic prostatectomy

key_events.event: Patient positioned in lithotomy with steep Trendelenburg
Diagnosis of compartment syndrome in right arm

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in lithotomy with steep Trendelenburg position during prolonged surgery
decision_points.prompt: When should the patient be repositioned during the surgery?

options.description: Reposition patient between the fourth and fifth hour of surgery
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard of care requires a positional holiday to prevent complications

options.description: Do not reposition patient until surgery is completed
options.clinical_reasoning: Prolonged positioning increases risk of compartment syndrome

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant did not reposition patient during the 9-hour surgery

expected_action_court.description: Patient should have been repositioned between the fourth and fifth hour
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony established this as standard practice

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to reposition led to compartment syndrome
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Patient should have been given a positional holiday
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed compartment syndrome requiring additional surgery

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to reposition patient during surgery
Causation established - failure to reposition led to compartment syndrome

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to reposition during prolonged surgery
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Repositioning would have prevented or lessened compartment syndrome

complications.complication: Nerve damage and limited use of right hand
